coredata.dc:title: The fabrication of excitation-dependent fluorescence boron/nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots and their employment in bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Karadag, Sugra Naz
Ustun, Oguzhan
Yilmaz, Asli
Yilmaz, Mehmet
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high quantum yield (QY) and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission is of great importance and provides unquestionable opportunities in biomedical applications. In this study, we have proposed a novel strategy for the fabrication of boron (B) and nitrogen (N)-co-doped CQDs (B/N-CQDs) by a simple yet facile hydrothermal method. For this, for the first time, dopamine and sodium borohydride were employed as C/N and B sources, respectively. CQDs were synthesized with excitation-dependent emission nature and high QY (25 %, the highest). The B/N-CQDs exhibited low toxicity and good biocompatibility through the cell viability tests by using MRC5 and A549 cell lines with IC50 values of 981 and 583 ppm, respectively. Lastly, the incubation of B/N-CQDs with an A549 cell line led to the emergence of blue and green colors under an excitation wavelength of 405 and 488 nm, respectively, indicating the excitation-dependent nature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co-doped carbon quantum dots
Bioimaging
Excitation-dependent emission
Hydrothermal synthesis
Dopamine
Sodium borohydride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of B/N-CQDs under different experimental conditions
Bioimaging via confocal laser scanning microscope

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of reaction temperature on UV–vis and PL spectra of B/N-CQDs. 180 °C (a-b), 200 °C (c-d), and 220 °C (e-f). The reaction time and the ratio of precursors were kept constant at 12 h and, 20:20 mM/mM, respectively.

The effect of reaction time on UV–vis and PL spectra of B/N-CQDs. 6 h (a-b), 12 h (c-d), and 18 h (e-f). The reaction temperature and the ratio of precursors were kept constant at 200 °C and, 20:20 mM/mM, respectively.

The effect of the ratio of precursors on UV–vis and PL spectra of B/N-CQDs. 30:10 mM/mM (a-b), 20:20 mM/mM (c-d), and 10:30 mM/mM (e-f). The reaction temperature and time were kept constant at 200 °C and, 12 h, respectively.

Further characterization of B/N-CQDs. Optic images in daylight and a 365 nm UV lamb (a), normalized PL intensity (b), TEM image and particles size distribution (c), Raman spectra (d), FT-IR spectra (e), XPS survey spectra with elemental ratio (f), high-resolution C1s (g), O1s (h), N1s (j) and B1s (k) spectra, the change in PL intensity (l) and UV–vis spectra (m) CQDs stored under daylight and dark conditions.
MTT assay of the A549 and MRC5 cells after incubation with B/N-CQDs for 24 h.

Confocal images of A549 cells treated with 250 ppm of CQDs under 405 and 488 nm excitation for different exposure times. The cells which were not treated with B/N-CQDs were selected as controls. Scale bars are 50 µm.

head.ce:title: The fabrication of excitation-dependent fluorescence boron/nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots and their employment in bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Molecular Biology and Genetics, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Molecular Biology and Genetics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Molecular Biology and Genetics, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Ataturk University, 25240 Erzurum, Turkey (M. Yilmaz).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high quantum yield (QY) and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission is of great importance and provides unquestionable opportunities in biomedical applications. In this study, we have proposed a novel strategy for the fabrication of boron (B) and nitrogen (N)-co-doped CQDs (B/N-CQDs) by a simple yet facile hydrothermal method. For this, for the first time, dopamine and sodium borohydride were employed as C/N and B sources, respectively. CQDs were synthesized with excitation-dependent emission nature and high QY (25 %, the highest). The B/N-CQDs exhibited low toxicity and good biocompatibility through the cell viability tests by using MRC5 and A549 cell lines with IC50 values of 981 and 583 ppm, respectively. Lastly, the incubation of B/N-CQDs with an A549 cell line led to the emergence of blue and green colors under an excitation wavelength of 405 and 488 nm, respectively, indicating the excitation-dependent nature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last two decades, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have arisen as a novel carbon-based nanomaterial and attracted significant attention worldwide [1]. CQDs are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with very small sizes and significantly high fluorescence activity [2,3]. In addition, these carbon nanosystems exhibit highly desirable and tunable features such as extraordinary optical properties, high water solubility, high stability, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and eco-friendly nature [4]. With these unique properties, CQDs have become promising and robust candidates for wide applications including bioimaging, biosensing, chemical sensing, nanomedicine, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, and solar cells [5–15]. Furthermore, CQDs have superior features in comparison to traditional QDs in terms of optical properties, chemical inertness, photochemical stability, low toxicity, and, high biocompatibility [16]. These unique features make them more suitable, especially in biomedical applications [17]. For the fabrication of CQDs, both the top-down and bottom-up strategies are utilized by using different precursors [1,4,18]. In the top-down approach, the relatively larger carbon materials such as graphite, graphene, etc. are broken down into small pieces through various processes including arc discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical oxidation [19–24]. The requirement of high energy and complicated device, poor control over the process, and resultant CQDs are the main drawbacks of this strategy that has to be considered. However, in the bottom-up approach, small organic molecules are used as precursors and CQDs are synthesized through carbonization-condensation via thermal treatment [25]. Pyrolysis, microwave irradiation, combustion, solvothermal and hydrothermal treatments are commonly used methods as bottom-up strategies [25,26]. The bottom-up strategies provide noticeable advantages in terms of simple and flexible synthesis procedures, low cost, easily accessible precursors, and short process time. Of these strategies, the hydrothermal method is the most preferred one since it is easy, cheap, and has experimental parameters such as time, temperature, and pressure that can be manipulated easily. These properties enable to tune the optical properties of CQDs through adjustable synthesis conditions [27,28].

Despite the excellent characteristics of CQDs and recent progress in synthesis procedures, limited fluorescence activity with low quantum yield (QY, less than 10 %) and optical stability are major drawbacks that must be considered [29,30]. Surface passivation or functionalization and element doping are the most commonly used methods to overcome these difficulties [31–34]. The surface passivation or functionalization modification strategies require an additional and time-consuming procedure, produce CQDs with larger sizes, and are not feasible on a large scale [19,35]. The doping with heteroatoms such as boron, nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, etc., is the most effective and easiest method to eliminate such limitations by introducing new energy levels and functional groups [36]. The emergence of new surface groups creates trap states with different energy levels resulting in the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission [19,33,37]. In heteroatom doping, boron (B) and nitrogen (N) are mostly utilized elements due to their noticeable advantages. Firstly, B and N are the adjacent neighbors of the C in the periodic table. Secondly, both B and N have an atomic size similar to C. Lastly, the substitution of C by B or N dramatically changes the local chemical reactivity, which enhances the optical performance [38–42]. Although there have been some reports on the fabrication of B/N co-doped CQDs (B/N-CQDs), further studies are still required to clarify the phenomena behind these nanosystems. Also, these CQDs with high QY and an excitation-dependent emission nature would provide unquestionable benefits in biomedical applications.

In this study, we proposed a simple yet facile hydrothermal method for the synthesis of B/N-CQDs. For this, dopamine and sodium borohydride were utilized as C/N and B source, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time dopamine and sodium borohydride have been employed in the synthesis of CQDs. We systematically investigated the effect of synthesis parameters such as synthesis temperature, reaction time, and the ratio of precursors on UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of resultant B/N-CQDs. For all runs, CQDs were fabricated with high QY and excitation-dependent emission characteristics. The B/N-CQDs obtained in optimized conditions were furtherly characterized through electron microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The stability of B/N-CQDs was examined by collecting UV and fluorescence spectra at certain times from the samples stored under daylight and dark conditions at room temperature. Also, the cytotoxic effect of the as-prepared CQDs was determined by using human lung carcinoma (A549) and human healthy lung cell line (MRC5). A549 cells were incubated with a certain amount of B/N-CQDs for different times for bioimaging with a confocal microscope at different excitation wavelengths. The cytotoxicity test and confocal imaging indicated the low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and suitability of the proposed nanosystem for biomedical applications.
Dopamine hydrochloride (98 %) and, sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized (DI) water with a conductivity of 0.05 µS/cm was used in all experimental procedures.

The UV–visible absorption spectra were collected from a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV–1800). The fluorescence spectra were measured through an Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. A Bruker VERTEX 70v instrument was used to record IR spectra (400–4000 cm−1). An XPS (Specs-Flex) was employed to evaluate the surface states of B/N-CQDs. The morphology and size of CQDs were determined through a TEM (Hitachi HighTech HT7700) operated at 120 kV. MTT assay was determined via a Biotek Epoch microplate reader by measuring the absorption at 570 nm. For bioimaging of CQD exposed cells, a Zeiss LSM 710 confocal laser scanning microscope was used at different laser excitation wavelengths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of B/N-CQDs under different experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
Briefly, a certain amount of dopamine and NaBH4 was dissolved in 10 mL of DI water. In this study, we investigated the effect of various experimental parameters such as the molar ratio of dopamine to NaBH4, hydrothermal synthesis temperature, and time on the fluorescence activity of resultant CQDs. The molar ratios of dopamine to NaBH4 were selected as 10:30, 20:20, and 30:10 mM/mM. The reaction temperature and times were in the range of 180–220 °C and 6–18 h, respectively. All hydrothermal syntheses were carried out in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor. After the reaction, the reactor was left at the ambient condition to cool to room temperature. The resultant suspension was centrifuged three times at 14,500 rpm for 30 min to remove large aggregates. Lastly, the CQDs were dialyzed through a dialysis membrane with an MW cut-off of 1 kDa for 24 h for the removal of unreacted impurities. Quinine sulfate was used as a standard (ϕ st = 0.54) to calculate the fluorescence quantum yields (ϕ) of B/N-CQDs. The following equation was employed to determine ϕ.
(1)
ϕ =
ϕ
st
( I /
I
st
)
( η /
η
st
)
Here, ϕ represents the QY, I is the slope of the curve, and η represents the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “st” denotes standard [43–45].

To evaluate the cytotoxic effect of as-prepared B/N-CQDs on cells, a standard MTT assay was implemented [46]. A549 and MRC5 cells were cultured in 96 well plates (103 cells per well). The plates were incubated in a humidified incubator at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The aqueous suspension of B/N-CQDs was added to the cell culture medium to obtain different final concentrations from 31 to 1000 ppm. The cells grown in a regular growth medium were donated as the negative control, and cells treated with a proper amount of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 250 µM) were considered as the positive control. After a 24 h incubation time, each well was rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and a proper amount of MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide salt) was added to the medium. Then, the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Lastly, the media was removed and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. To determine the cell viability, the optical densities at 570 nm were measured by using a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging via confocal laser scanning microscope
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence imaging of the B/N-CQDs was investigated in A549 cell lines. For this, 5 × 104 A549 cells were grown on a coverslip in a 24 mm culture dish and incubated in a 5 % CO2 humidified incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. After the attachment of cells, 250 ppm as-prepared B/N-CQDs was added to each well and incubated with different time periods (1, 2, and 3 h). Then, the cells were rinsed with PBS two times and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde at 37 °C for 10 min. At the end of the fixation, the cells were washed with PBS several times and kept in PBS. For the confocal imaging, coverslips were taken onto glass slides and observation was performed under the different excitation wavelengths at 405 and 488 nm. For the negative control, unexposed cells were imaged under the same experimental condition.

B/N-CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal carbonization method. In the synthesis protocol, we investigated the effect of some experimental parameters such as reaction temperature, time, and the ratio of precursors on optical properties (fluorescence and absorbance). In the following section , the optical properties of all CQDs were measured in the UV–vis and PL spectra and discussed in detail.

In the first step, we manipulated the reaction temperature in the range of 180–220 °C at constant reaction time and precursor ratio. After each synthesis, UV–vis and PL spectra were collected to determine optical characteristics of the resultant CQDs (Fig. 1 ). From UV–vis absorption spectra, we detected a weak shoulder for all resultant CQDs at around 220 nm indicating π-π* transition band of aromatic CC bonds (Fig. 1a,c,e). UV–vis spectra showed that the shoulder from the π-π* transition band becomes more prominent as the temperature increases and the center of the shoulder shifts towards a higher bandgap. In addition, at 180 °C due to the low carbonization, the shoulder is almost invisible. Also, a prominent peak at around 280 nm was observed which was attributed to the n–π* transition of CO bonds or other surfaces groups [3,47]. There are no significant differences at 200 and 220 °C in the peaks for this transition. However, B/N-CQDs synthesized at 180 °C were incomplete with lower stability. Therefore, the resultant n–π* transition peak was more intense [48]. Lastly, a broad shoulder peak in the range of 380–390 nm was noticed due to the presence of lower energy states as a result of N-related functional-surface groups [37,49,50]. This broad peak showed a blue shift as the temperature was increased significantly (Fig. 1a, c, e). To evaluate the fluorescent properties of the B/N-CQDs, we performed a detailed PL study with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 420 nm (Fig. 1b, d, f). The surface groups of B/N-CQDs have trap states with different energy levels resulting in an excitation-dependent behavior at all temperatures. The fluorescence maxima were ranged from 406 nm to 470 nm with the increased excitation wavelengths. For the synthesis performed at 180 °C (Fig. 1b), relatively lower fluorescence activity was noticed in comparison to higher temperatures (Fig. 1d,f). This issue was mainly attributed to the lower carbonization at a lower temperature. However, the increase in reaction temperature led to a remarkable enhancement in fluorescence emission. No noticeable change was detected in between 200 and 220 °C runs. It seems that at these temperatures the precursors were sufficiently carbonized to create a satisfactory fluorescence activity.

In the second step, the effect of the synthesis time on optical properties was investigated in detail. For this, similar to the previous step, UV–vis and PL spectra were collected and similar results were observed for different reaction times (Fig. 2 ). Similarly, π-π* transition band of aromatic CC bonds, n–π* transition of CO bonds or other surfaces groups and a broad shoulder peak due to the presence of lower energy states as a result of N-related functional-surface groups was detected at around 220, 280 and 380 nm, respectively (Fig. 2a,c,e). Additionally, an extra absorption band was observed at 254 nm in the UV–vis spectrum for an 18 h reaction time (Fig. 2e). This peak indicates that another n–π* transition band was formed in the structure of B/N-CQDs, which created a different energy state [30,51]. In the PL spectra, the resultant CQDs exhibited the excitation-dependent characteristics for all reaction times with fluorescence maxima ranging from 405 nm to 472 nm under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2b, d, f). The increase in reaction time resulted in a significant improvement in fluorescence emission. It is obvious that the prolongation of the reaction time led to favorable carbonization of the precursors and resultant satisfactory fluorescence activity (Fig. 2d, f). The highest PL emission for the case of 18 h reaction time (Fig. 2f) may be attributed to the emergence of an extra absorption band due to the enhanced carbonization.

Lastly, the effect of the ratio of the precursor on optical properties was investigated in detail through UV–vis and PL spectra (Fig. 3 ). In the UV–vis spectrum, similar characteristics were observed in the peak originating from the N-related surface groups (Fig. 3a,c,e). In the PL spectra, the resultant CQDs exhibited the excitation-dependent characteristics for all reaction times with fluorescence maxima ranging from 408 nm to 480 nm under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3b, d, f). When the amount of dopamine decreased and the amount of NaBH4 increased, a significant decrease in the ratio of the N-related peak occurred (Fig. 3e). This issue created a remarkable decrease in the intensity of resultant PL spectra. The highest spectra was attained for the 20:20 mM/mM ratio of dopamine to NaBH4 (Fig. 3d). To determine the effect of co-doping on optical properties, we performed some analyses for the case of only N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs, NaBH4 was not employed as precursor). For the case of N-CQDs, the peak of the surface-related groups was disappeared in UV–vis spectra (see Fig. S1a). Also, the PL spectra of N-CQDs showed significantly reduced excitation-dependent properties in comparison to B/N-CQDs (see Fig. S1b). This analysis reveals the importance of B doping in the formation of surface-functional groups and resultant optical properties. It seems that both N and B elements are prerequisites for the fabrication of CQDs with high PL activity.

After evaluation of reaction temperature, time, and the ratio of precursors on optical properties (fluorescence and absorbance), further analyses were performed for CQDs prepared at the pre-determined conditions.

For further characterization of CQDs, we performed various measurements and analyses in detail. For this, B/N-CQDs were synthesized at 200 °C of temperature, 12 h reaction time, and 20:20 mM/mM of precursor ratio. Some of the basic properties of the fluorescent CQDs were summarized in Fig. 4 . B/N-CQDs also exhibited excellent solubility in water with a pale brownish-yellow color in daylight. However, a bright blue emission was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Figura 4a). As described in the previous section, the UV–vis absorbance and PL properties of CQDs are highly dependent on the synthesis conditions. In the normalized fluorescence emission spectra, the excitation dependence of CQDs was obviously detected under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4b). The increase of the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm led to a redshift (longer emission wavelength) in fluorescence emissions. In addition, CQDs exhibited a strong fluorescence with a good quantum yield of 25 % by using quinine sulfate solution as a reference (see Fig. S2). This value is highly correlated with various CQDs in the literature (see Table S1). The morphology and size of the B/N-CQDs were examined by TEM. TEM image and histogram which was given as an inset show that the B/N-CQDs are mostly spherical with an average diameter of 9.8 nm and broad distribution in the size range of 5–14 nm (Fig. 4c). For the comparison, we collected TEM images of CQDs prepared at different temperature (220 °C) under the same synthesis time and precursor ratio (see Fig. S3). We obtained a more homogeneous size distribution (5–14 nm) with a similar average size (9.6 nm). This issue may be attributed to higher carbonization at high temperature. The investigation of carbon characteristics was evaluated via the Raman spectroscopy technique and the corresponding Raman spectrum was given in Fig. 4d. Raman spectra exhibited two broad peaks at 1338 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to the d-band and G-band, respectively [52]. The D band was related to disorders due to the sp3 defects, and the G band was associated with the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the lattice. The ratio of integral areas of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) was found to be 0.47. This value showed the high content of defects and disorders compared to graphite [53–55]. For the comparison, we also provided the Raman spectra of dopamine and sodium borohydride in Fig. S4. No Raman bands associated with the precursors were detected in the spectra indicating the purity of the samples and total carbonization of the precursors. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to figure out the molecular structure and chemical composition of B/N-CQDs. (Fig. 4e). For B/N-CQDs, the absorption bands at 3224, 2921, 1608, 1488, 1448, 1223, 1098, 1053 and 906 cm−1 were assigned to υ (OH/NH), υ (BOH), δ (CC), υ (BO), υ (CH), υ (CO), υ (BO3/CN), υ (COC) and υ (BCO2), respectively [56,57]. The hydrophilic groups such as υ (OH/NH), υ (BOH) and, υ (CO) prove the excellent solubility of the as-prepared CQDs in water [58–60]. Also, the bands for δ (CC), υ (CH) and, υ (CN) groups indicated the presence of polyaromatic nature of the CQDs’ core [61,62]. We also collected FT-IR spectra of dopamine and sodium borohydride for comparison (Fig. S5). These spectra showed the carbonization of the precursors through hydrothermal synthesis. XPS analysis was carried out on B/N-CQDs to deepen the study of their surface composition and bonding structures. The survey spectra of B/N-CQDs exhibited four prominent peaks at around 192, 285, 400, and 532 eV corresponding to B1s, O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. The elemental percentage of the material composed of B/O/N/C was found to be 2.3/23.0/6.1/68.6 (Fig. 4f). It is obvious that the hydrothermal procedure enabled the co-doping of the B and N in the structure of the CQDs to a different extent. The high-resolution XPS spectrum core level of C1s was convoluted to four peaks at 284.5, 285.6, 286.4, and 289.91 eV which were corresponded to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 4g). The presence of the CO and CO bonds is evidence for carbonyl groups [57]. O1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 4h) was fitted with two major peaks at 531.6 and 532.5 eV which were assigned to CO and CO, respectively. N1s spectrum (Fig. 5 j) showed three main peaks at 398.7, 400.0, and 401.1 eV which were corresponded to N (C)3, CN-C, and NH, respectively [63]. B1s spectrum (Fig. 5k) was deconvoluted into four component peaks centered at 190.9, 191.8, 192.5, and 193.4 eV. The component peaks were attributed to the BC, BN/BCO2, CBN, and BO bonds, respectively [64]. In conclusion, the data and analyses showed that CQDs consist of graphite core with the high content of defects and disorders and both B and N are co-doped in the structure of the CQDs to a different extent.

Irradiation of CQDs with daylight may result in degradation to some extent [65]. Therefore, we further investigated the stability of the as-prepared B/N-CQDs under daylight and dark conditions. We collected PL spectra for different time intervals for the CQDs stored at different conditions (see Fig. 4l and S6). It was detected that the PL intensity of the sample stored in daylight was decreased sharper than that of stored in dark (Fig. 4l). The optical images of the samples under a 365 nm UV lamp clearly demonstrated the decrease in PL emission (Fig. S7a). Also, at the end of 15 days, the color difference between the samples can be observed with the naked eye (Fig. S7b). In the UV–vis spectra, it was detected that the broad peak around 385 nm (corresponded to N-related surface groups) was disappeared in the sample exposed in daylight compared to the freshly-prepared sample. No significant change was noticed for the sample stored in the dark conditions (Fig. 4m and S6b). Also, in the UV–vis spectra, a new shoulder was observed at around 310 nm for both samples stored in daylight and dark (Fig. S8). This issue basically may be attributed to the emergence of structural changes in B/N-CQDs. Taken together, CQDs were observed to be more stable in the dark. In addition, the changes in UV–vis spectra were associated with the decrease in fluorescence intensity. This suggests that the structure of CQDs and the linkage of PL features should be furtherly investigated.

The cytotoxicity of nanoparticles determines their potential for application in the biomedical field. Thus, in our case, a standard MTT assay was performed to investigate the toxic effect of B/N-CQDs on both A549 and MRC5 cell lines. The cell viability of the cells after 24 h incubation with different concentrations B/N-CQDs in the range of 31.2–1000 ppm has been assessed. As shown in Fig. 5, B/N-CQDs showed more toxic effects on the cancer cell line (A549) than the healthy cell line (MRC5). This phenomenon may provide unique advantages in the elimination of cancer cells through various modalities. Cell viability at 500 ppm was 76 % for MRC5 and 57 % for A549 indicating the low toxicity and good biocompatibility of the B/N-CQDs. The IC50 values for MRC5 and A549 cell lines were found to be 981 and 583 ppm, respectively. These results were highly compatible with the other type of CQDs given in the literature (see Table S2).

The low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and excellent optical properties of CQDs make them a desirable candidate for bioimaging. For this, we employed the A549 cell line and monitored them under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 6 , the A549 cells treated with 250 ppm of B/N-CQDs for 1–3 h exhibited blue and green color under an excitation wavelength of 405 and 488 nm, respectively. As mentioned earlier, this phenomenon is originated from the excitation-dependent nature. We detected that the accumulation and PL emission of the CQDs can be manipulated by tuning the exposure time. The results of the cytotoxicity test and confocal imaging show the low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and suitability of the proposed nanosystem for biomedical applications.

In summary, we investigated the synthesis of B/N-CQDs with a simple hydrothermal method and their employment in bioimaging. The as-prepared nanosystems showed bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light. Due to the B/N co-doping, the as-prepared B/N-CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent properties. The synthesis temperature, reaction time, and the ratio of precursors played a significant role in the optical properties of B/N-CQDs. For the selected experimental conditions, the CQDs yielded a high QY value of 25 % and exhibited high stability. The cell viability tests by using MRC5 and A549 cell lines indicated the low toxicity and high biocompatibility of the proposed nanosystem with IC50 values of 981 and 583 ppm, respectively. The excitation-dependent nature of B/N-CQDs led to blue and green color emission under different excitation wavelengths in bioimaging studies of the A549 cell line. We strongly believe that this report would pave the way for the usage of the B/N-CQDs in future applications.

Sugra Naz Karadag: Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology. Oguzhan Ustun: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Software. Asli Yilmaz: Supervision, Visualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Mehmet Yilmaz: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Ataturk University Scientific Research Project Coordination Unit (AU-BAP, No: FAB-2021-9302). The authors also kindly acknowledge the East Anatolia High Technology Application and Research Center (DAYTAM) at Ataturk University for providing laboratory spaces and characterization devices.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemphys.2022.111678.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A mini review on carbon quantum dots: preparation, properties, and electrocatalytic application
Front. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, Y. Feng, P. Dong, J. Huang, A mini review on carbon quantum dots: preparation, properties, and electrocatalytic application, Frontiers in Chemistry, (2019) 671.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Future perspectives and review on organic carbon dots in electronic applications
ACS Nano

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Semeniuk, Z. Yi, V. Poursorkhabi, J. Tjong, S. Jaffer, Z.-H. Lu, M. Sain, Future perspectives and review on organic carbon dots in electronic applications, ACS nano, 13 (2019) 6224-6255.
